**Clinical Reasoning:**
The patient exhibits classic features of Cushing’s syndrome: central obesity, hypertension, hyperglycemia, proximal muscle weakness, and absent cortisol suppression on high-dose dexamethasone. Serum ACTH is elevated (>5 pg/mL), indicating secondary hypersecretion—most commonly due to an ectopic source such as small cell lung cancer. The persistence of cortisol elevation without suppression confirms ectopic ACTH production. Prioritizing detection of the tumor source, especially given the patient’s heavy cigarette consumption—a major risk factor for lung cancer—is paramount. Chest imaging (MRI or CT) will target the most common anatomical origin of ectopic ACTH. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) is reserved for determining unilateral versus bilateral disease after localization and is not required initially.
